skill.name: agent-skill-trust-check
skill.description: Static pre-install trust review for agent skills, SKILL.md packages, MCP-linked bundles, and marketplace listings before installation.
skill.summary: Review agent skill text for risky permissions, missing provenance, and prompt-boundary concerns before installation.
skill.author: Tate Programs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but most confirmed cases are detection rules, documentation, template literals, or an explicit risky fixture rather than live behavior. The marketplace entry point reads stdin only, but the package also includes a local CLI that can read user-supplied paths and fetch allowlisted GitHub or Gist URLs, so publication should include a clear capability warning.

medium_findings.title: Local CLI Can Read Files And Fetch Remote Skill Text
medium_findings.description: The package exposes a local CLI that accepts a path or an allowlisted GitHub/Gist URL, then reads or fetches that target before analysis. This is legitimate for a trust-review tool, but it is broader than the stdin marketplace runner and should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads a user-supplied local path and fetches allowlisted remote URLs. The host allowlist and absence of secret exfiltration lower the severity, but the capability is real.

medium_findings.title: Published Manifest Understates Package-Level Capabilities
medium_findings.description: The SKILL.md permissions block declares network, filesystem, and subprocess access as false for the skill interface, while the repository also ships a package CLI with local file reads and remote fetch support. The safer stdin runner is documented, but marketplace reviewers should distinguish it from the npm CLI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The stated permissions match the stdin interface, but package installation also exposes another executable path. This is a documentation and trust-boundary issue rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Strings Are Mostly Scanner Rules Or Documentation
low_findings.description: Many static hits for shell commands, destructive commands, secrets, weak prompt boundaries, and network output are regex rules or explanatory text used by the analyzer. They are not executed by the skill and should be treated as expected content for a security-review utility.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged patterns appear inside rule definitions and documentation describing what to detect. No evidence shows these strings are executed as commands.

low_findings.title: Bundled Risky Fixture Contains Injection And Exfiltration Examples
low_findings.description: The example fixture contains text about API keys, pipe-to-shell installation, webhooks, background cron, and ignoring previous instructions. The file states it exists to test the scanner, so this is a controlled fixture, not an operational instruction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious phrases are real, including prompt-boundary language, but the surrounding context identifies them as a sample target for validation. This reduces confidence that it is a malicious prompt injection attempt.

low_findings.title: Template Literals And Markdown Code Fences Caused Command-Execution False Positives
low_findings.description: Static findings labeled Ruby or shell backtick execution mostly correspond to JavaScript template literals, inline code, or fenced command examples. These are output formatting and usage documentation, not shell evaluation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are visible in console output strings and Markdown examples. I did not find evidence that these backtick-delimited strings invoke a shell.

dangerous_patterns.title: Regex Signatures Match Dangerous Behavior Terms
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer intentionally searches for shell execution, destructive commands, credential access, network output, persistence, and prompt-boundary language. These signatures explain the high static score but are part of the product behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The rule table explicitly defines these signatures. The semantic context confirms they are detection patterns rather than actions performed by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sample Target Includes High-Risk Skill Claims
dangerous_patterns.description: The fixture intentionally models a skill that reads an API key, pipes a remote installer to bash, posts to a webhook, installs cron, and asks the agent to ignore previous instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The high-risk text is present and useful for testing scanner output. The file-level note says it is only a scanner test, so it is not evidence of runtime behavior.

content.value_statement: Installing third-party agent skills can expose shell access, secrets, wallets, or private project data. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users a structured static review before they install or publish a skill.
actual_capabilities: Reviews SKILL.md text, marketplace descriptions, and install instructions before installation.
Flags shell execution, destructive commands, credential access, payment actions, network output, persistence, and prompt-boundary language.
Reports missing provenance signals such as license, source, version, tests, permissions, and uninstall notes.
Returns a verdict, risk score, findings, positive signals, missing signals, and patch order.
Provides a stdin runner for marketplace environments that should avoid local file reads and network fetches.
Offers a local CLI for reviewing local files or allowlisted public GitHub and Gist URLs.
limitations: It performs static text review only and does not prove runtime safety.
It can produce false positives when dangerous terms appear in examples or documentation.
It does not replace dependency review, sandboxing, runtime monitoring, or manual code review.
The local CLI can read supplied paths and fetch allowlisted URLs when users choose that mode.

use_cases.title: Pre-Install Skill Review
use_cases.description: Check an unknown agent skill before giving it local, account, network, wallet, or tool access.

use_cases.title: Marketplace Submission Screening
use_cases.description: Find unclear permissions, missing provenance, and risky examples before submitting a skill to a marketplace.

use_cases.title: Reviewer Triage
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable first-pass trust report before deeper manual security review.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a single SKILL.md file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Agent Skill Trust Check to review this SKILL.md text. Give me the verdict, top findings, missing signals, and recommended patch order.

prompt_templates.title: Check A Marketplace Listing
prompt_templates.scenario: Review visible listing copy before purchase or install.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this marketplace listing for skill trust risk. Treat missing implementation details as unknown and list what I should verify before installation.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare A Seller Patch List
prompt_templates.scenario: Help a creator improve a listing before publishing.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this skill description and produce a prioritized patch order that addresses unsafe behavior, unclear permissions, and missing provenance.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these agent skill listings for trust posture. Rank them by install risk and explain which missing signals or dangerous patterns drive the ranking.

output_examples.input: A SKILL.md file mentions shell commands, webhooks, and no license.
output_examples.output: The report marks the skill as review before install, highlights shell and network concerns, and asks for license, permissions, and uninstall details.

output_examples.input: A marketplace listing includes source, version, tests, permissions, and no sensitive actions.
output_examples.output: The report notes positive provenance signals and may mark the skill reasonable to trial in a sandbox.

output_examples.input: A seller wants to reduce rejection risk before publishing.
output_examples.output: The report gives a patch order focused on dangerous behavior first, then documentation gaps such as tests and rollback steps.

best_practices: Use the stdin runner for marketplace automation when local file reads and network fetches are not needed.
Review the generated findings manually before blocking or approving a third-party skill.
Ask sellers to document permissions, network destinations, tests, versioning, source, license, and uninstall steps.
anti_patterns: Do not treat a low static score as proof that a skill is safe at runtime.
Do not execute an unknown skill while performing the pre-install review.
Do not ignore risky behavior just because the listing has a polished description.

faq.question: Does this skill execute the target skill?
faq.answer: No. It performs a static text review and does not run the target skill.

faq.question: Why can it flag safe documentation?
faq.answer: It searches for risky terms, so examples and rule descriptions can trigger findings that need human review.

faq.question: What is the safest entry point?
faq.answer: The stdin runner is safest for marketplaces because it reads provided input and returns JSON without local file reads or URL fetches.

faq.question: Can it review public GitHub skill files?
faq.answer: Yes. The local CLI can fetch allowlisted GitHub, raw GitHub, and Gist URLs when users choose that mode.

faq.question: Does it replace a full security audit?
faq.answer: No. It is a first-pass trust review and should be paired with code review, sandboxing, and dependency checks.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: AI tool users, skill creators, and marketplace reviewers can use it before installing, publishing, or approving agent skills.
